coredata.dc:title: On chip manipulation of carbon dots via gigahertz acoustic streaming for enhanced bioimaging and biosensing
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Miaosen
He, Shan
Pang, Wei
Wei, Wei
Zhou, Feng
Wu, Xiaoyu
Qi, Hang
Duan, Xuexin
Wang, Yanyan
coredata.dc:description: Currently, researches on nanomaterials have been restricted by slow and multistep synthesis procedures. Herein, we demonstrate an ultrafast, one step method of purification and delivery of quantum dots into living cells, actuated by the acoustic streaming (AS) produced through a gigahertz resonator. Results demonstrate that the impurities in the carbon dots (CDs) can be extracted immediately aided by the acoustic forcing, with extra high purification efficiency of 93%. The system can also efficiently deliver the CDs into cells, showing excellent nucleus and mitochondria uptake under 3 min of AS treatment, and making the organelles of cells to be recorded more easily and simultaneously. More importantly, the AS is found to further accelerate the bioreaction inside the cells, thus realizes the enhanced biosensing of Fe3+ in single living cells. This work develops a novel type of multifunctional method for effective purification, intracellular delivery and biosensing of nanomaterials, inspiring the biological/medical nanotechnology researches at subcellular level.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots (CDs)
Acoustic streaming (AS)
Cellular delivery
Ferric detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant numbers 61971302 ), and Nanchang Institute for Micro-technology of Tianjin University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the purification and intracellular delivery of CDs for bioimaging and Fe3+ sensing.

(a) Schematic illustration of the CDs purification under the effect of AS. (b) Simulation results of AS velocity field. (c) Microscopy images of SMR driving acoustic streaming in the microfluidic channel and capturing impurities. The gold pentagon in the figure is the top electrode of the SMR. (scale bar = 100 μm). (d) Representative atomic force microscopy (AFM) images and thickness profiles of CDs before (left) and after AS (right). (e) Lateral diameter measurements distributions of CDs before and after AS. (f) Average thickness distributions of CDs before and after AS. (g) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs under different excitation wavelengths (Dot line: UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Schematic illustration of the intracellular delivery of CDs. (b) Fluorescence images (brightness: +40%, scale bar = 250 μm) of HeLa cells incubated with CDs (5 mg/mL) for 3 min (left), 45 min (center) and treated with AS for 3 min (right). (c) Comparison of the cellular fluorescence intensity with different treatment. (d) Fluorescence images (scale bar = 250 μm) of HeLa cells treated with CDs (5 mg/mL) under 3 min AS of different power (0, 100, 300, 500, 700, and 800 mW). (e) Linear relationship of the applied power and intercellular fluorescence intensity of CDs.

Intracellular distribution of CDs under the 800 mW AS. (a) HeLa cells with nuclear stain, CDs (5 mg/mL, 800 mW AS), merged images and fluorescence intensity along the red line in the merged image (scale bar = 30 μm). (b) HeLa cells with mitochondria stain, CDs (5 mg/mL, 800 mW AS), merged image and fluorescence intensity along the red line in the merged image (scale bar = 30 μm). (c) 3D-view of HeLa cell with nuclear and CDs stain. (d) x–z slice along the line displayed in (c). (e) 3D-view of HeLa cell with mitochondria and CDs stain. (f) x–z slice along the line displayed in (e). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) HeLa/CDs with different concentration of Fe3+ (0, 0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.40, and 1 mM) for 3 min with or without 500 mW AS (scale bar = 250 μm). (b) Statics analysis of the average F/F
0 fluorescence with or without AS. (c) Microscopy images of CDs droplet (0.1 mg/mL) reacting with the Fe3+ (0, 0.20, 0.50 and 1 mM) with or without 500 mW AS for 30 s. (d) Average F/F
0 statistics of the droplet experiments.

Microscopy image (left, scale bar = 30 μm) and 3D-view (right) of HeLa/CDs (a) before and (b) after treated with Fe3+ (1 mM, 500 mW AS for 3 min). (c) Fluorescence intensity along the red line in (a, b). (d) Statics analysis of the average F/F
0 fluorescence in 0.1 mg/mL CDs solution, the nucleus and the cytoplasm of cells before and after reacting with 1 mM Fe3+. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: On chip manipulation of carbon dots via gigahertz acoustic streaming for enhanced bioimaging and biosensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Precision Measuring Technology and Instruments, College of Precision Instruments and Opto-electronics Engineering, Tianjin University, Tianjin, 300072, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Precision Measuring Technology and Instruments
College of Precision Instruments and Opto-electronics Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Precision Measuring Technology and Instruments, College of Precision Instruments and Opto-electronics Engineering, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contribute equally to this article.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Currently, researches on nanomaterials have been restricted by slow and multistep synthesis procedures. Herein, we demonstrate an ultrafast, one step method of purification and delivery of quantum dots into living cells, actuated by the acoustic streaming (AS) produced through a gigahertz resonator. Results demonstrate that the impurities in the carbon dots (CDs) can be extracted immediately aided by the acoustic forcing, with extra high purification efficiency of 93%. The system can also efficiently deliver the CDs into cells, showing excellent nucleus and mitochondria uptake under 3 min of AS treatment, and making the organelles of cells to be recorded more easily and simultaneously. More importantly, the AS is found to further accelerate the bioreaction inside the cells, thus realizes the enhanced biosensing of Fe3+ in single living cells. This work develops a novel type of multifunctional method for effective purification, intracellular delivery and biosensing of nanomaterials, inspiring the biological/medical nanotechnology researches at subcellular level.

An original multifunctional acoustic streaming actuator was fabricated with a gigahertz resonator, which facilitated ultrafast purification, cellular internalization of carbon dots as well as accelerating the intracellular reaction especially in nucleus.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a new class of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials with size below 10 nm, have attracted significant attention in recent years [1,2]. Due to the unique quantum confinement and edge effect together with negligible photobleaching and toxicity properties [3], CDs are drastically different from semiconductor quantum dots or metal nanodots. They have been explored for possessing excellent properties in photoluminescence, electrochemical properties, catalytic activity, photostability and ease in preparation [4–7], rendering them shown outstanding potential for biosensing [8–10], bioimaging [11], biomedicine [12,13], photothermal therapy [14,15], enzymatic detection [16] and energy [17]. Despite these achievements, however, researches on CDs are still at early stage, and some key challenges need to be further resolved for bringing the CDs into the real world applications [9]. For instance, although there have emerged many new routes to fabricate CDs in the past few years, the CDs are typically synthesized and purified by time-consuming multistep procedures involving repeated centrifugation cycles and long-time dialysis. In addition, the relatively low quantum yield [18], poor cell uptake efficiency [19,20] and large variation of sensing outcome [21] also hinder the further bio-application of CDs. Therefore, it is in high demand to exploit hand free and rapid strategies for quantum dots treatment to make up for the shortcomings of current methods.

Microfluidic systems have been developed to date for on chip sorting, preconcentration, mixing and delivery of micro/nano particles [22–24]. A recently discovered acoustofluidic system, combining the microfluid with acoustics has shown great potential in hand free and time efficient treatment of microparticles [25–29]. To construct the acoustofluidic platform, piezoelectric resonator in various configurations has been applied to transduce acoustic wave into fluid to generate acoustic streaming (AS) in microfluidic devices for further application, including surface acoustic waves (SAW) [30,31], ultrasound transducer [32] et al. Among these, bulk acoustic waves (BAW), due to the distinct advantages in ultra-high frequency of gigahertz level, high acoustic energy conversion rate and miniaturization, have shown to be a powerful tool for fluidic actuation [33–35]. It has recently been confirmed that the strong acoustic force driven by BAW can realize in-situ mixing and microparticles concentrating [36,37]. In addition, recent works from our group also found that this strong acoustic streaming could drive the membrane of mammalian cells [38] as well as inducing deformation [35] and differentiation of cells [39], to facilitate the biological applications.

Here we demonstrated high-efficiency purification and ultrafast intracellular delivery of CDs in the BAW based acoustic streaming platform. The as purified CDs showed in more uniform sizes, and more sensitive fluorescence response to Fe3+. More importantly, this platform increased 15.8-fold delivery efficiency of CDs, which provided a powerful tool for intracellular drug delivery and cellular imaging. This platform was further found to promote the entry of ions into nucleus and lead to the acceleration of intracellular reaction kinetics between CDs and Fe3+. This work provides a feasible strategy for ultrafast purification of nanomaterials and a high-performance biosensing platform for intracellular sensing. Given the CMOS (complementary metal-oxide semiconductor) compatible and microscale of BAW device, the concept in this work could potentially facilitate future high throughput bioimaging and biosensing applications.

Calcium chloride dihydrate (CaCl2·2H2O, 99.7%), chromium chloride hexahydrate (CrCl3·6H2O, 98%), lead chloride (PbCl2, 99.7%), iron chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O, 99%), manganese chloride (MnCl2, >99%), and magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl2·6H2O, 99.7%) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co.,Ltd. (China). All the chemicals were used as received without further treatment. The polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) precursor and curing agent (Sylgard 184) were obtained from Dow Chemical Corporation (USA). Carbon dots (CDs, 10 mg/mL, aqueous solution) were purchased from Suzhou Xingshuo Nanotech Co.,Ltd (China). All aqueous solutions were prepared using Millipore Deionized (DI) water. The HeLa cell line and NIH/3T3 cell line were obtained from American Type Culture Collection (ATCC, USA). Fetal bovine serum (FBS), Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM), MitoTracker™ Deep Red FM, and NucRed™ Live 647 ReadyProbes™ were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific (USA). Phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH = 7.4), propidium iodide (PI) and calcein-AM were purchased from Solarbio (China). All experiments were conducted at room temperature.

The GHz solid mounted thin film bulk acoustic resonator (SMR) was fabricated by standard CMOS compatible process [40]. Briefly, bragger reflector layers composed of three groups of aluminum nitride (AlN) and silicon dioxide (SiO2) were deposited on undoped silicon wafer by plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition (PECVD) and reactive sputtering, respectively. Then, a 600 nm molybdenum (Mo) layer was deposited by radio-frequency (RF) sputtering and patterned as the bottom electrode by plasma etching. Following, aluminum nitride film (1500 nm) with a crystal orientation along the c-axis was deposited as the piezoelectric layer by reactive magnetron sputtering. Finally, the resonator was evaporated and patterned with Au (50 nm) that served as a top electrode.

PDMS microchannels with different height were prepared from silicon mold (width: 200 μm, length: 4 mm). The microchannels and resonator were cleaned by ultrasonic and plasma cleaner, and bonded together to form an acoustofluidic platform. The capillary (tetrafluoroethylene capillary, 0.3 mm inner diameter, China) connecting with syringe pumps (Harvard Apparatus, Pump 11, USA) was inserted into the inlet of microchannel for solution injection. The acoustic waves were excited by applying a sinusoidal signal of 1.58 GHz to resonator with a radio frequency signal generator (KEYSIGHT, EXG Analog Signal Generator, N5171B 9 kHz–6 GHz, USA) and a power amplifier (Mini-Circuits, ZHL-5W-422+, USA).

The acoustofluidic platform was fixed on the stage of upright fluorescence microscope (BX53, Olympus) and the manipulation process of CDs was recorded with CCD camera (DP73, Olympus) at excitation wavelength of UV band. Briefly, 0.5 mL of raw CDs solution was continuously introduced into the microchannel (height: 70 μm), and collection of purified CDs solution started 0.5 min after turning on the resonator (input power: 10 mW). CDs were characterized with fluorescence spectrometer (Jobin Yvon Inc., Jobin Yvon Fluorolog3-21, France) and atomic force microscope (AFM, Bruker Nano Surfaces, NanoWizard 4, Germany). For the sensing test of Fe3+, FeCl3 powder was dissolved in DI water to prepare Fe3+ stock solution, and the ions of Ca2+, Cr3+, Pb2+, Mn2+ and Mg2+(1 mM) were utilized for the selectivity detection. The fluorescence emission spectra of the above mixed solutions were measured under 405 nm excitation to keep consistent with the laser used in confocal microscope for cell imaging.

HeLa cells and 3T3 cells were cultured in complete DMEM medium with 10% fetal bovine serum at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 atmosphere. Before experiment, cells (8 × 104/mL) were seeded on glass slides and pre-cultured for 24 h. Then, the slides with cells were placed on the top of PDMS microchannel and made sure that the device located in the center of the slide. After injecting as prepared CDs solution (in DMEM medium, 5 mg/mL) into PDMS channel (height: 1.5 mm), different power of resonator was applied (0, 100, 300, 500, 700, and 800 mW) for entire duration of 3 min. The treated cells were then rinsed with PBS for 3 times and imaged.

To explore the distribution of CDs in cell, nuclear and mitochondria of live cells were stained with NucRed™ Live 647 and MitoTracker™ Deep Red respectively for 20 min, followed by delivery of CDs into cells with 800 mW input power. After that, cells were rinsed with PBS for 3 times and fixed with paraformaldehyde. All cell experiments were imaged by a confocal microscope (Leica, TCS SP8). The 3D images were obtained by confocal microscope with 63 × oil immersion objective lens with 1 μm interval along Z direction. The X-Z image was obtained by a cross-section on the 3D image in the confocal microscope software.

After delivering CDs under 800 mW AS, the HeLa cells were rinsed with PBS for 3 times, Fe3+ solution with concentrations of 0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.40, and 1 mM were successively incubated with cells for 3 min with or without acoustic streaming (input power: 500 mW). After each incubation, the HeLa cells were rinsed three times with PBS to remove extracellular Fe3+, then the fluorescence images of cells were captured by confocal microscope.

In order to evaluate the effect of AS on the reaction of CDs and Fe3+, a drop of CDs solution (30 μL, 0.10 mg/mL) reacting with different concentration of Fe3+ (0, 0.20, 0.50 and 1 mM) on the surface of resonator for 30 s with and without acoustic streaming was designed. The fluorescence images of the droplets were captured by upright fluorescence microscopy.

The fluorescence quantitative analysis in the experiment was carried out using ImageJ software. Identical parameters of edge extraction algorithm and denoising algorithm were utilized to analyze the same batch of fluorescence images. The average fluorescence intensity is defined as the ratio of the total fluorescence intensity of the denoised image to the area of cells. All experiments were performed at least three times (n = 3), and the results are reported as average fluorescence intensity ± standard deviations.

The schematic of system in this work is exhibited in Fig. 1 , which is composed of two PDMS channels and piezoelectric resonator devices. The 1.58 GHz resonator, fabricated by MEMS technology, can convert the electrical energy into mechanical vibration under the inverse piezoelectric effect to form the sound energy at gigahertz (GHz) frequency. When such acoustic waves are coupled from solid substrates to microfluidics, the wave energy absorbed by liquid will bring flow of the fluid, thus triggering the AS. AS is the result of acoustic energy flux dissipation within the fluid, its force is strongly dependent on the applied power of resonator and the distance from resonator surface [35,39,41]. In this work, we found that by selecting the appropriate microchannel height, the system could achieve excellent functions in selectively capturing larger size of particles and deforming the cell membrane for intracellular delivery of CDs.

To understand the quantum dots manipulating behavior under such GHz acoustic streaming, the raw CDs obtained by hydrothermal method, the most popular approach for the synthesis of CDs, were pumped into the microchannel firstly (Fig. 2 a). The size distribution of raw CDs is in a broad range from 10 nm to 140 nm (Fig. 2e black histogram). Traditional purification procedure of centrifugation is limited in this size range, making them in poor optical and chemical properties. Wide size distribution of CDs had serious influences on the intracellular delivery and cell viability, thus confined their further bioimaging and biosensing applications. The schematic of Fig. 2a gives the experimental setup employed for the CDs purification. When resonator stimulated the liquid in the microchannel, the boundary significantly restricted the radius of the vortex, a group of high-speed micro vortices was created. The fluid forces that vortex act on particles include the viscous force (F
v ) along the direction of the fluid velocity tangent and the inertia force (F
I ) along the direction of the fluid velocity gradient (Fig. S1 a). The expressions of viscous and inertial forces are shown in equations (1) and (2), respectively.
(1)
F v
= 3 π μ υ D
(2)
F I
= R e ⋅
F v
In the equation, μ is the viscosity of the liquid, υ is the fluid velocity, D is the particle diameter, and Re is the Reynolds number.

It can be known from the equation that the strength of the fluid force on the particles is linearly related to the particle diameter. The smaller the particle size, the less fluid force it is subjected to. As the raw CDs solution containing different sizes of particles was pumped into the microchannel and pass through the resonator device, the bigger size of CDs was more likely to be trapped into the vortex, thus the purification of CDs could be realized. The finite element simulates the working process of acoustic streaming effect in this system which is exhibited in Fig. 2b. It clearly shows the small vortices are formed above the resonator, and the velocity distribution of micro vortices are distance dependent. By optimizing the microchannel with height of 70 μm, the behavior of raw CDs materials flowing through the microchannel embedded with resonator were recorded under microscopy (Fig. 2c). The CDs solution passed through the channel without any interference when the resonator was power-off (Fig. 2c left), but when the resonator was power-on, strong AS formed, the bigger-size impurities were trapped in the vortices (Fig. 2c right), while the smaller-size quantum dots passed through the channel and were collected (the detailed image and video information are provided in Fig. S1 b, Video S1 and Video S2). In this way, the purification of CDs was realized.
Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2022.123462.

The following are the supplementary data related to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1
Multimedia component 2 Multimedia component 2

To evaluate and quantify the as purified CDs, the collected solution was further examined by atom force microscopy (AFM, Fig. 2d). It clearly observed that the average diameter of CDs decreased dramatically after treatment by AS (Fig. 2e), the removal rate of impurities (lateral size >15 nm) was up to 93% (Fig. S1 c). We also observed the resultant CDs thinned from average 1.5 to 1.2 nm, indicating the ability of AS treatment to reduce the dimension of CDs (Fig. 2f). The GHz AS technique constitutes an efficient method for the generation of thinned layer CDs, which provides a new tool for 2D materials reduction.

The optical properties of the obtained CDs were further characterized with photoluminescence (PL) method (Fig. 2g). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs exhibited a typical absorption peak. When exciting the CDs at different wavelength ranging from 340 to 420 nm, the peak intensity of emission spectra increases continuously. However, with further increase of excitation wavelength, the peak intensity of emission spectra startes to decrease, indicating the purified material exhibits a quantum dots nature, and the excitation dependent fluorescence emission with a maximum occurs at an excitation of 420 nm, which is in consistent with the literatures [42].

Co-incubating the CDs with cells to promote them enter a cell by endocytosis-based uptake path ways is a common method to study the biological properties of CDs. But it always needs a long incubation time from several hours to days [13,43–45]. Our previous results have reported that the shear stress generated by the acoustic streaming could act on the membrane of cells and produce temporary pore on the membrane [38], that facilitates the foreign materials cross the cell plasma membrane and directly enter the cytoplasm (Fig. 3 a). As shown in Fig. 3b, when incubating the CDs with HeLa cells directly, no obvious green fluorescence of CDs appeared, suggesting that few CDs entered cells. In contrast, upon exposure of AS, HeLa cells displayed strong fluorescence intensity. Importantly, the entire duration of AS treatment was only 3 min, and causing 15.8-fold enhanced fluorescence intensity compared with that of 3 min co-incubation (Fig. 3c). The influences of resonator input power on the delivery of CDs were further studied. The fluorescence field images of Fig. 3d revealed that more CDs had entered cells when higher power of resonator was applied, and there was a linear relationship between the applied power and the fluorescence intensity of cells (Fig. 3e). Among these, the most obvious green fluorescence was in the 800 mM group, and 100% cells exhibited strong green fluorescence of CDs, suggesting that the acoustic streaming did trigger CDs enter cells in an ultra-fast way, which provided a highly efficient tool for drug delivery study of nanomaterials. As demonstrated by molecular probe of Calcein-AM (live cell staining, green fluorescence) and propidium iodide (PI, dead cell staining, red fluorescence) (Fig. S2), the AS exhibited no obvious toxicity to HeLa cells, and thus ensured its suitability for further bioimaging and biosensing studies.

Inside of a typical cell, different intracellular organelles perform specific biological functions and represent hallmarks of disease, thus bioimaging intracellular organelle is vital for medical diagnosis [46]. As a newly-emerging fluorescent reagent, the limitation of CDs for bioimaging is that most of as prepared CDs can only locate in the cytoplasmic area [20,44,47–49], as reasons of their broad size range and barrier of nuclear membrane. Utilizing the excellent intracellular delivery properties of AS, CDs can be delivered into all structures of cells. Further co-localization experiments were conducted to precisely specify the bio-distribution of CDs inside the cell after AS. The nucleus and mitochondria staining were performed after the AS treatment immediately, to prove whether the CDs could accumulate inside the nucleus or mitochondria. From the confocal microscope images of Fig. 4 a, it could be found that green fluorescence of CDs was well overlapped with the red fluorescence of NucRed™ Live 647, representing that CDs distributed inside the nucleus. Especially, the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the nucleus region was much stronger than that in the cytoplasm, confirming that under the influence of AS, the majority of CDs moved into the nucleus within 3 min, showing potential application to trace the nuclear changes in living cells. Mitochondria are also major intracellular targets for nanoparticles payloads, as shown by the Fig. 4b, the confocal images of CDs and MitoTracker™ Deep Red supported that the CDs were located in the mitochondria, verifying the CDs could cross the mitochondrial membrane within 3 min facilitated by AS. 3D confocal imaging further clarified the location of CDs in cells, indicating that the CDs were well distributed throughout the entire HeLa cells (Fig. 4c). The enhanced bioimaging of cells were further confirmed by 3T3 cell lines (Fig. S3). Similarly, CDs entered nucleus of 3T3 cells and emitted green fluorescence.

The above results suggest that under action of AS, the CDs can cross the cellular barriers and accumulate in the cellular organelles at high concentration to realize intracellular bioimaging. Importantly, the present system uses only resonator as actuator without any additional surface functionalization procedure, which is safe to the biological samples. Furthermore, the entire procedure is one step and ultrafast, reducing the delivery time to just 3 min, along with a superior effect to that obtained via conventional methods.

Fe3+, as an essential trace element, has been demonstrated relevant to cancer disease [50,51]. Finding a simple and sensitive method for Fe3+ detection in living cells is vital to the early diagnosis of disease. It is known that the ion of Fe3+ can specific covalent binding with the amine groups of CDs and result in fluorescence quenching of CDs [52]. As the spectra demonstrated in Fig. S4 a, the CDs can selectively and sensitively bond with Fe3+. The fluorescence intensity of CDs was significantly quenched and decreased to 34.9% with 1 mM of Fe3+, while no obvious changes in the fluorescence quenching value (F/F
0 ) of CDs were observed after adding other metal ions (including 1 mM of Ca2+, Cr3+, Pb2+, Mn2+ and Mg2+, Fig. S4 b), suggesting that the CDs is an ideal candidate for selective ferric sensing. The fluorescence spectra of CDs interaction with different concentration of Fe3+ were studied (Fig. S4 c). The fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased gradually with increasing the concentration of Fe3+, and a good linear relationship between the F/F
0 and concentration of Fe3+ (from 0.01 to 1 mM) was observed with a detection limit of 10 μM (3 times signal-to-noise, S/N), demonstrating that the CDs can be utilized for intracellular Fe3+ detection in the coexistence of various metal ions with the high selectivity and sensitivity.

Encouraged by the excellent properties of sensing Fe3+ of CDs, the intracellular Fe3+ sensing was then performed. After 3 min delivery of CDs, the HeLa cells were exposed to freshly prepared culture medium containing 0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.40 and 1 mM Fe3+ for 3 min co-incubation or AS treatment. As revealed by the confocal images, the fluorescence intensity of CDs in HeLa cells of both groups was decreased as increasing the concentration of Fe3+ (Fig. 5 a and b), indicating that the CDs realized Fe3+ detection at the cellular level. Specifically, the F/F
0 of AS treatment group was found declining more rapidly comparing with the co-incubation group without AS, suggesting that the AS may also accelerate the reaction of CDs and Fe3+.

To verify the above assumption, droplet-based experiment was conducted. By dropping 30 μL CDs solution on the surface of resonator, the fluorescence images were captured after reacting with 0.20, 0.50 and 1 mM of FeCl3 solution with or without 30 s AS. The considerable quenching was also evident in this experiment (Fig. 5c and d). Both of the microscopy images and F/F
0 results illustrated that the quenching behavior was accelerated with AS compared to that of without AS. Under the AS treatment, the fluorescence signal of CDs was quenched to the value when CDs reacted completely with Fe3+. Comparatively, the CDs still maintained at a high level after 30 s with co-incubation methods. The reaction rate of CDs and Fe3+ was approximately 2-fold enhanced with the AS than that of without AS, indicating superior mixing ability of AS, that the high localized AS promotes the ions full access to CDs, leading to an acceleration of reaction kinetics and causing ultra-fast fluorescence quenching in the droplet. These data also illustrated that the AS not only have function in promoting the intracellular delivery, but also can accelerate the intracellular reaction, which provides a promising way for the intercellular phenomena study.

To further verify the accelerated intracellular reaction of AS, the fluorescence changes in cytoplasm and nucleus before and after reaction with ferric were investigated in detail. By comparing cells before and after the reaction with 1 mM Fe3+, the results in Fig. 6 showed that the fluorescence quenching value of cells decreased to approximately 35% by the quenching of Fe3+, either in nucleus or cytoplasm, of which the value was same as the extracellular experiment, revealing that the superior performance of AS can deliver the ions into nucleus and accelerate their reaction inside immediately. This observation manifests that the integration of microfluidic approach with AS showed potential for imaging and sensing in single living cells.

In summary, we developed a novel multifunctional AS platform, in which the AS acted as an actuator, combined with microchannel and enabled one-step, ultrafast purification and extracellular delivery of CDs in living cells. In purification process, reduced dimension and thinned layers of CDs were obtained under the action of AS. In delivery process, it was found that the AS could deliver the CDs into nucleus and mitochondria within 3 min, and did not interfere with the viability of cells, offering high-efficiency assay without requiring modification processes. In particular, we also proved that the application of AS accelerated the intracellular reaction between CDs and Fe3+ especially in nucleus, which made it a better cellular ions detection method. Thus, this work discovers a novel strategy for reducing the intracellular delivery time to an ultrafast 3 min. The study in this work will facilitate a better understanding of the bio-application of CDs, and the strategy may provide a new guidance for the biological/medical nanotechnology research.

Yanyan Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing, Project administration, Supervision. Miaosen Zhang: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing. Shan He: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Editing. Wei Wei: Investigation. Feng Zhou: Investigation. Xiaoyu Wu: Editing. Hang Qi: Editing. Wei Pang: Resources. Xuexin Duan: Resources. Miaosen Zhang and Shan He contributed equally to this article.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant numbers 61971302), and Nanchang Institute for Micro-technology of Tianjin University.

J. Am. Chem. Soc.

Carbon
